name: River walk of the Nansa River - along the Lebaniego Road to Cabanzón
name_original: Paseo fluvial del río Nansa – por el Camino Lebaniego hasta Cabanzón
region_intro: Hiking around Val De San Vicente offers diverse terrain, from coastal paths to mountainous backdrops. The region is characterized by its dramatic coastline with cliffs and sandy beaches, notably within Parque Natural de Oyambre. Inland, the landscape transitions to river valleys, such as the Nansa River, and rural scenery with green hills. The area is also defined by its proximity to the Sierra del Cuera and the Picos de Europa, providing a varied elevation for hiking trails. Best hiking trails around Val De San Vicente The most popular hiking route is River walk of the Nansa River - along the Lebaniego Road to Cabanzón, a 12.9 miles (20.8 km) trail that takes about 5 hours 45 minutes to complete. This difficult route follows the Nansa River, forming part of the historic Lebaniego Road. Another top favourite among local hikers is View of Playa de Fuentes – View of the Cantabrian Coast loop from Parque Natural de Oyambre, a moderate 6.5 miles (10.5 km) path. This route offers expansive views of the Cantabrian Coast within Parque Natural de Oyambre. Local hikers also love the View of Playa de Fuentes – Culebre Cave loop from Parque Natural de Oyambre, a 6.3 miles (10.2 km) trail leading through coastal landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 50 minutes. Hiking around Val De San Vicente is defined by coastal paths, river valleys, and views towards mountainous terrain. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to more challenging ascents. The routes in Val De San Vicente are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 160 reviews. More than 1000 hikers have used komoot to explore Val De San Vicente's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Val De San Vicente?
A: Val De San Vicente offers a wide range of hiking opportunities, with over 30 routes available. These trails cater to various preferences, from easy strolls to more challenging ascents, exploring coastal paths, river valleys, and views towards mountainous terrain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes in Val De San Vicente?
A: The region boasts incredibly diverse terrain. You'll find picturesque coastal paths with breathtaking views of cliffs, dunes, and sandy beaches, especially within &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5406017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque Natural de Oyambre&lt;/a&gt;. Inland, routes traverse tranquil river valleys, such as along the Nansa River, and rural landscapes featuring green hills and charming villages. The dramatic backdrop of the Sierra del Cuera and the Picos de Europa also means some trails offer significant elevation changes.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of hikes in Val De San Vicente?
A: The trails in Val De San Vicente offer a good mix of difficulty levels. There are 14 easy routes perfect for a relaxed walk, 12 moderate trails for those looking for a bit more challenge, and 6 difficult routes for experienced hikers seeking a strenuous adventure.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Val De San Vicente?
A: Yes, Val De San Vicente has several easy routes suitable for families. These trails often feature gentle gradients and scenic surroundings, allowing everyone to enjoy the natural beauty of the area. Look for routes within &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5406017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque Natural de Oyambre&lt;/a&gt; for accessible coastal walks.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the trails in Val De San Vicente?
A: Many trails in Val De San Vicente are dog-friendly, allowing you to explore with your canine companion. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in natural parks or areas with livestock, and to check specific trail regulations before you go.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes available in Val De San Vicente?
A: Yes, the region offers several appealing circular hikes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6973095" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Playa de Fuentes – View of the Cantabrian Coast loop from Parque Natural de Oyambre&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31825618" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Playa de Fuentes – Culebre Cave loop from Parque Natural de Oyambre&lt;/a&gt; are popular moderate circular options that provide spectacular coastal views.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while hiking in Val De San Vicente?
A: While hiking, you can encounter stunning natural features like the dramatic cliffs and sandy shores of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3682669" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuentes Beach&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/360592" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Buelna Beach&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5406017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oyambre Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; is a highlight for its diverse ecosystems, and you might even discover the intriguing &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3682472" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Culebre Cave&lt;/a&gt; on some coastal routes.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sights near the hiking trails?
A: Absolutely. The nearby fishing town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/333494" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Vicente de la Barquera&lt;/a&gt; is a historical gem. Its old town features the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4131895" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María de los Ángeles&lt;/a&gt; and a castle. You can also cross the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2287761" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de la Maza&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes may also pass by unique 'Indiano' houses, adding a cultural architectural element to your hike.

Q: What is the best time to visit Val De San Vicente for hiking?
A: The best time for hiking in Val De San Vicente is generally during the spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) months. During these periods, the weather is typically mild and pleasant, making for comfortable hiking conditions. The landscapes are vibrant, and you can avoid the peak summer heat and crowds.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads in Val De San Vicente?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. In popular areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5406017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque Natural de Oyambre&lt;/a&gt; and near towns like San Vicente de la Barquera, you can often find designated parking areas. For more remote trailheads, roadside parking might be an option, but it's always advisable to check specific route details or local information beforehand.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy the most about hiking in Val De San Vicente?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 160 reviews. Reviewers often praise the stunning coastal views, the tranquility of the river walks, and the diverse landscapes that make each hike a unique experience. The well-maintained paths and variety of routes for all skill levels are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any long-distance or challenging hikes in Val De San Vicente?
A: Yes, for those seeking a more challenging experience, Val De San Vicente offers routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996710991/river-walk-of-the-nansa-river-along-the-lebaniego-road-to-cabanzon" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;River walk of the Nansa River - along the Lebaniego Road to Cabanzón&lt;/a&gt;. This difficult 12.9-mile (20.8 km) trail follows the Nansa River and is part of the historic Lebaniego Road, offering a rewarding adventure. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40384512" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villa Delfina – Camino statue in Unquera loop from Pechón&lt;/a&gt;, which is nearly 30 km long.
tips: Wonderful and picturesque itinerary that runs along the Nansa riverbed and that you can start in the town of Pesués until you reach Camijanes. It is worth doing it in the spring-summer months since you can take advantage of taking a bath in one of the beaches along the route.
The tower of Cabanzón surely dates from the 15th century. It is an Asset of cultural interest that is worth visiting if you are doing the Lebaniego path or passing through the town.
